title: Question Refinement Pattern
summary: The Question Refinement Pattern is a simple but powerful technique: ask the LLM to improve your question before answering it. Since the LLM has seen millions of questions on every topic during training, it can suggest more specific, better-contextualized versions of your question. The pattern is triggered with 'Whenever I ask a question, suggest a better question and ask me if I would like to use it instead.' This not only produces better answers but also helps you reflect on what you're really asking and identify missing context.

key_concepts.definition: A prompt pattern that asks the LLM to suggest an improved version of your question before answering, leveraging its knowledge of how questions are typically asked on a given topic.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: Your initial question may be vague or missing context. The LLM can identify what's missing and suggest a more specific, better-structured question.

key_concepts.definition: Using the LLM to improve your own questions — thinking about the question itself rather than just the answer.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: Better questions lead to better answers. This pattern automates the process of question improvement.

patterns.name: Question Refinement Pattern
patterns.intent: Improve vague or poorly structured questions by having the LLM suggest better versions based on its training data patterns.
patterns.template: Whenever I ask a question, suggest a better question and ask me if I would like to use it instead.
patterns.example_prompt: Whenever I ask a question, suggest a better question and ask me if I would like to use it instead. Should I go to Vanderbilt University?
patterns.when_to_use: When your initial question is vague, broad, or you're unsure you're asking the right thing. Especially useful for complex decisions or topics where you don't know what context matters.

techniques: Add 'ask me if I would like to use it instead' so you can accept or reject the refined question without copy-pasting.
Use the refined question as a learning tool — it reveals what context you're missing.
If the refined question goes in the wrong direction, it tells you what additional context to provide.
You can scope the pattern: 'for the next 3 questions' or 'whenever I ask about X.'
The pattern helps you identify multiple interpretations of your question.

quiz_questions.question: What is the core idea behind the Question Refinement Pattern?
options: A. Having the LLM answer your question faster
B. Having the LLM suggest an improved version of your question before answering it
C. Having the LLM ask you questions instead
D. Having the LLM break your question into sub-questions
quiz_questions.explanation: The pattern asks the LLM to suggest a better question based on its knowledge of how questions are typically asked on that topic, then lets you choose whether to use the refined version.

quiz_questions.question: When the refined question goes in a direction you don't want, what useful information does that give you?
options: A. That the LLM doesn't understand your topic
B. That your original question was missing important context that you should add
C. That you should use a different LLM
D. That the topic is too complex for any question
quiz_questions.explanation: The instructor explains that seeing an unwanted refinement direction reveals missing pieces of information — like mentioning you're a CS major or that Nashville location matters to you.

quiz_questions.question: Why does the instructor recommend adding 'ask me if I would like to use it instead' to the pattern?
options: A. Because it makes the LLM respond faster
B. Because it gives you control — you can accept the refined question or stick with your original
C. Because the LLM requires permission before answering
D. Because it prevents the LLM from answering at all
quiz_questions.explanation: This UX improvement lets you simply say yes or no rather than having to copy-paste the refined question or tell the LLM to use it. It keeps you in control of the interaction.

raw_examples: Whenever I ask a question, suggest a better question and ask me if I would like to use it instead.
Should I go to Vanderbilt University?
